title: Entanglement Witnesses Quiz
description: Test your understanding of entanglement witnesses and their role in detecting quantum correlations.

questions.question: What is the defining property of an entanglement witness W?
options: Tr(W * rho) = 0 for all entangled states
Tr(W * rho) >= 0 for separable states, and Tr(W * rho) < 0 for some entangled states
Tr(W * rho) < 0 for all entangled states
W must be a positive semidefinite operator
questions.explanation: An entanglement witness W satisfies: (1) Tr(W*rho) >= 0 for all separable states, and (2) there exists at least one entangled state with Tr(W*rho) < 0. A negative expectation value proves entanglement, but a non-negative value doesn't prove separability.

questions.question: Geometrically, what does an entanglement witness represent?
options: A point inside the separable set
The center of the entangled set
A hyperplane separating some entangled states from the separable set
The boundary of all quantum states
questions.explanation: In the convex geometry of quantum states, an entanglement witness corresponds to a hyperplane that separates certain entangled states from the convex set of separable states. The witness equation Tr(W*rho) = 0 defines this hyperplane.

questions.question: For a witness W = alpha*I - |psi><psi| constructed from an entangled state |psi>, what is alpha?
options: The smallest eigenvalue of |psi><psi|
Always equal to 1/2
The maximum overlap of |psi> with any product state, squared
The entanglement entropy of |psi>
questions.explanation: Alpha = max_{|phi> product} |<phi|psi>|^2 is the maximum overlap with product states. For a two-qubit state, this equals the largest Schmidt coefficient squared. This ensures Tr(W*rho_sep) >= 0 for all separable states.

questions.question: If a witness gives Tr(W*rho) = 0.1 > 0, what can we conclude?
options: The state rho is definitely separable
The state rho is definitely entangled
We cannot conclude whether rho is entangled from this witness alone
The witness W is invalid
questions.explanation: A non-negative witness value does not prove separability - it only means this particular witness doesn't detect the state's entanglement. The state might still be entangled but require a different witness to detect it.

questions.question: What is a decomposable witness?
options: A witness that can be measured in a single experiment
A witness of the form W = P + Q^TB where P, Q are positive semidefinite
A witness constructed from multiple entangled states
A witness that detects all entangled states
questions.explanation: A decomposable witness can be written as W = P + Q^TB where P and Q are positive semidefinite and TB denotes partial transpose. These witnesses can only detect NPT (negative partial transpose) states and cannot detect PPT entangled states.

questions.question: Why are entanglement witnesses experimentally valuable?
options: They always give exact entanglement measures
They require fewer measurement settings than full tomography
They work without any quantum measurements
They can create entanglement in the lab
questions.explanation: Witnesses are experimentally valuable because they can certify entanglement with far fewer measurements than full quantum state tomography. A witness decomposed into Pauli observables might need only 4-10 settings compared to 4^n - 1 for n-qubit tomography.

questions.question: When decomposing the Bell state witness into Pauli operators, which terms appear with non-zero coefficients?
options: Only II
II, XX, YY, and ZZ
All 16 two-qubit Pauli operators
Only ZZ
questions.explanation: The Bell state witness W = (1/2)I - |Phi+><Phi+| decomposes as (1/4)(II - XX + YY - ZZ). Only four Pauli terms appear, meaning only four measurement settings are needed experimentally.

questions.question: What is required to detect bound entanglement (PPT entangled states)?
options: Any entanglement witness will work
Decomposable witnesses
Non-decomposable witnesses
Bound entanglement cannot be detected by witnesses
questions.explanation: Bound entangled states have positive partial transpose (PPT), so decomposable witnesses (which rely on partial transpose) cannot detect them. Non-decomposable witnesses are required, though constructing them is more difficult.
